steps.title: Understand what would weaken the hypothesis
steps.content:
The political scientists hypothesized that municipalities respond more and offer more incentives when an announcement comes before an election than after. To weaken this, the graph data must show no meaningful difference between the two timing groups across those outcomes.
  • Choice A — A large majority of the municipalities that received an inquiry mentioning plans for an announcement before the next election didn't respond to the inquiry.: Describes only the pre-election nonresponse rate without any comparison to the post-election group — cannot weaken the hypothesis alone. ✗
  • Choice B — The proportion of municipalities that responded to the inquiry or offered incentives didn't substantially differ across the announcement timing conditions.: Directly shows that timing made no difference — both before- and after-election groups responded and offered incentives at nearly identical rates, contradicting the hypothesis. ✓
  • Choice C — Only around half the municipalities that responded to inquiries mentioning plans for an announcement before the next election offered incentives.: Describes internal rates within only the pre-election group, with no cross-group comparison. ✗
  • Choice D — Of the municipalities that received an inquiry mentioning plans for an announcement date after the next election, more than 1,200 didn't respond and only around 100 offered incentives.: Describes only the post-election group without comparing it to the pre-election group. ✗

steps.title: Confirm and conclude
steps.content: The correct answer compares both timing conditions and shows no substantial difference, directly undermining the hypothesis that pre-election timing drives higher response rates. That confirms B is correct.
